course_description:
Research project/thesis report on topic in research field as recommended by supervisor and approved by Head of School.

This course offers comprehensive and substantial research training. Students seeking enrollment must obtain a recommendation from their supervisor and approval from the Head of School. To secure this approval, please submit your request to smp.student@uq.edu.au. The #8-unit research project courses --- MATH7012, MATH7030, MATH7031, STAT7013, STAT7018, and STAT7019 --- all follow the same structure and requirements. Courses MATH7030 and STAT7018 commence in Semester 1 and conclude at the end of the subsequent Semester 2; MATH7031 and STAT7019 commence in Semester 2 and conclude at the end of the following Semester 1. Meanwhile, courses MATH7012 and STAT7013 are designed for completion within a single semester (either in Semester 1, or Semester 2, but not in the Summer Semester).

This course involves focused study and research on a selected topic within Mathematics, including Applied Mathematics, Data Science, Mathematics Education, and Statistics, under the guidance of an academic staff member from the School of Mathematics and Physics. The principal component of the project entails reading and comprehensively understanding recent developments in mathematics and statistics research literature, followed by writing a detailed report or thesis. Depending on the chosen area of specialization, the project may involve applying established theories to novel problems, comparing various methodologies, or synthesizing scattered results into a coherent presentation. To identify an appropriate research topic, students typically consult with members of the Mathematics Discipline whose research aligns with their interests, subsequently selecting a topic that best fits their academic goals. Further details on ongoing research within the School are available from the postgraduate coursework coordinator. While producing original, publishable results is commendable, it is not a mandatory expectation at this academic level. Nevertheless, a high-quality project will offer valuable insights to mathematicians and statisticians by providing a clear and informative overview of contemporary research.

Throughout the duration of the project, students are required to:
timetable:
Additional timetable information
Students will usually meet weekly with their advisor.
course_aims: To read and understand recently developed mathematics and statistics from the research literature and ﾠto explain this material in a seminar and a written report or thesis.

learning_outcomes.description: Locate, synthesize, and critically assess contemporary mathematical and statistical research; identify relevant theories, methods, and open problems using advanced bibliographic tools and databases.

Formulate precise research questions, select or develop appropriate analytical, computational, or experimental techniques, and generate valid, reproducible results that withstand mathematical and statistical scrutiny.

Apply advanced mathematical reasoning, statistical inference, and efficient implementation skills (e.g., coding, simulation, numerical methods) to solve complex problems, justify conclusions, and assess limitations.

Deliver a well-structured seminar that informs and engages a general mathematical audience, employing clear logic, illustrative examples, and effective data visualisation. Produce a thesis that is coherent, rigorously argued, and appropriately referenced, situating the work within the broader discipline and comparing alternative approaches.

Articulate and justify methodological choices and findings in a formal defense; respond thoughtfully to critique, reflect on the project's significance and limitations, and propose meaningful directions for future research.

assessment_summary.due_date: 28/07/2025 - 31/10/2025; The precise date will be advised in due course.

assessment_details.title: Project Seminar
assessment_details.due_date: 28/07/2025 - 31/10/2025 The precise date will be advised in due course.
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The aim of the seminar is to explain the project to a general mathematical audience that includes all your fellow graduate students and members of staff. At least the first half of the talk should be understandable by everyone. The last few minutes may be a little more technical and explain the project’s contributions to experts. The seminar should last for about 20 minutes leaving about 5 minutes for questions. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The seminar should be video recorded. The video record could be submitted to the Blackboard system, or saved by the supervisor for one year.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
See ADDITIONAL ASSESSMENT INFORMATION for extension and deferred examination information relating to this assessment item.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item then the listed penalty will be deducted per day for up to 7 calendar days, at which point any submission will not receive any marks unless an extension has been approved. Each 24-hour block is recorded from the time the submission is due.

assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: There is no formal page limit, typically the thesis is up to 100 pages long. No formatting or referencing style is formally prescribed, but formatting and referencing should be clear, be used consistently, and be in accordance with the standard practices of the discipline. learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 8
learning_activities.topic: Progress Report Each student will submit a brief written progress report (usually about 20-30 pages long), including a literature review, to their advisor. The advisor and examiner will assess this report which will contribute 15% to the final mark for the project. In particular they should advise the student at this stage of any additional approaches of further literature that needs to be considered. The project report should contain (*) a substantially complete literature review, (*) a draft outline of the thesis. (e.g. chapters and titles), and (*) a realistic schedule of tasks to be completed in the remainder of the time. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Exam week 1
learning_activities.topic: Master Thesis The student will write a thesis under the guidance of the advisor. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13
learning_activities.topic: Masters Seminar Each student will give a seminar to the mathematics department. The seminar lasts 25 minutes, including 5 minutes for questions. The seminar counts 10% towards the overall mark. The aim of the seminar is to explain the project to a general mathematical audience; including all graduate students and members of staff. At least the first half of the talk should be understandable by everyone. The last few minutes may be a little more technical and explain the projects contributions to experts. Masters students will give their seminars in a series of weekly seminars in the maths department. The exact date will be notified early in the semester. Learning outcomes: L01, L03

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life.
